segment-text: Benjamin A. August Production Draft: White 06/11/2014
"Because I remember, I despair. Because I remember, I have the duty to reject despair."
Double White Rev (08-15-14) INT. BEDROOM - MORNING

ZEV, 90, opens his effervescent blue eyes with a shudder. He is drenched in sweat. He was having a bad dream. His eyes, a valley surrounded by deep mountainous wrinkles, attempt to adjust to the dim light. With an aged, deep voice, bearing thick traces of an Eastern European accent, the old man mutters a half question: Ruth? ZEV Zev slowly turns his head to the right, expecting to find his wife. However, nobody is there. He strains his eyes at the slightly creased pillow and tucked in sheet, trying to get a grasp on the situation. After a moment, he sits up and stares ahead at a mirror. Instead of seeing his aged reflection though, he looks at a white sheet. This room has been prepared for sitting shiva - the Jewish tradition of mourning.
INT. LIVING ROOM - DAY - MOMENTS LATER
A PIANO takes up a quarter of the small room. Like in the bedroom, the mirrors are covered with white sheets. PAULA, black, 40s, heavyset, Senegalese, knits a sweater on a worn-in, reclining chair.
Where is my wife?
Paula is startled, but it is Zev -- standing timidly in the bedroom doorway -- who is afraid.
Mr. Guttman! You can't be sneaking up on me like that!
Where is my wife? Where is Ruth?
Paula stands with a truly sympathetic look on her face.
I'm sorry, Mr. Guttman. Your wife passed away about a week ago.
Zev takes in this devastating information. Dead? A week ago? Zev takes a seat on the piano bench and begins to rub his forehead with his arthritic hands.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Who are you?
My name's Paula. I was Mrs. Guttman's nursing aide. Your family asked me to help out for another week or two.
(meekly) I'm ... a little confused.
It's okay, Mr. Guttman. You've been through a lot the last week.
Paula gives Zev the most compassionate smile she can muster, despite this being a conversation she's probably had before. Zev looks around the apartment as if he's a visitor and not the tenant.
Let's get some breakfast. You've been feeling better after breakfast.
Paula approaches Zev and takes his hand. He's as lost as a little child who's been separated from his parents.
INT. HALLWAY - DAY
There are few places in the world as sterile as the
hallways of a senior housing community. The olive green,

diamond patterned carpet has been vacuumed every day. The fluorescent overhead lights are without a trace of dust. The flowers on wooden tables by the elevator banks are as fake as the lithographs hanging above them. Zev and Paula exit the apartment. Zev walks with a slight shuffle but is more than able to get around by himself. CELE, nonagenarian, walks toward Zev, pushing a walker with tennis balls attached to the bottom. She smiles at him as she gets closer.
Good morning, Zev.
Good morning.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
I hope you're not too hungry. Breakfast is crummy as usual.
That's okay. I'm not too hungry. Cele continues her journey down the hallway. Zev gives her a second look. When Cele's out of earshot, Paula whispers:
That's Cele. She was friends with your wife.
(irritated) I know who she is. Like most people suffering from dementia, it's very easy
for Zev to become irritated and suffer mood swings.
INT. CAFETERIA - DAY

Zev and Paula sit at a long rectangular table, eating breakfast. The eggs, toast and fruit are separated on a compartmentalized plastic dish on an orange plastic tray. Most of the SENIORS in the cafeteria, like Zev, reside in the INDEPENDENT LIVING WING of the senior housing community. However, there are others who require full time CARETAKERS and reside in the ASSISTED LIVING WING. MAX, a feeble old man in a wheelchair, is one such man. He gets pushed up to the table by his AIDE. Max is on the opposite side of the spectrum as Zev; his body has gone to hell but his brain is extremely acute. There's an oxygen tank attached to Max's wheelchair. The necessity of it is quickly apparent as he struggles to breathe as he talks.
Can you -- give us -- a minute?
Max, like Zev, enunciates every word when he speaks -- an indication that English is their second language.
No problem, Max.
Zev shoots Paula an angry look with his piercing eyes.
You don't need to tell me everyone's name.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Paula and Max's aide gossip in their native tongue as they walk away. Max looks around to make sure the coast is clear.
Zev thinks about it but comes up blank.
Zev shakes his head in frustration. Max gathers his breath.
It's okay. (long breath) I wrote everything down -- so you could remember.
Max pats his jacket pocket, as if something is inside. He then starts to cough uncontrollably. His aide walks over and inserts the oxygen nose-clip into Max's nostrils.
Come on, Mr. Rosenbaum. Time for a nap. The aide takes Max away.
INT. ZEV'S APARTMENT - NIGHT

A MINYAN is gathered. It's a big crowd for a small apartment. ZEV'S CHILDREN, GRANDCHILDREN, GREAT GRANDCHILDREN and FRIENDS are all present and being led through the prayers by a RABBI. Zev wears black slacks, a white button down shirt and an old tweed professor's blazer with patches on the elbows. He is also the only one without their Siddur opened; this is not a man who believes in god. The adults -- sans Zev -- recite KADDISH. They've all got the prayer committed to memory.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
raba. B'al'ma di v'ra khir'utei. V'yam'likh mal'khutei
uv'chayei d'khol beit yis'ra'eil ba'agala uviz'man kariv v'im'ru. Amein...
INT. ZEV'S APARTMENT - NIGHT

The minyan is winding down. Family and friends finish their bagels and lox and say their goodbyes. Zev sits on the couch, receiving words of condolence as people make their way out of the apartment. His GREAT GRANDDAUGHTER, 8, sits next to him. Max gets wheeled up to the great granddaughter.

Do you know -- when that picture - was taken? Max shakily points at a BLACK AND WHITE WEDDING PHOTOGRAPH of Zev and RUTH, 20, beautiful. The photo stands on an end table, amongst family portraits. The great granddaughter shakes her head no. Max looks at Zev, who also doesn't seem to have a clue.
That is from -- their wedding day
Did you meet Nanny in Germany?
There's recognition in Zev's eyes.
No. I met her in America. I met her at Coney Island.
The great granddaughter looks transfixed at the picture.
She was beautiful, -- wasn't she? (off her nod) Why don't you -- show the picture -to your brother?
Double White Rev (08-15-14)

The great granddaughter excitedly takes the picture and walks across the room to her brother ADAM, who stands next to their MOTHER and FATHER. Zev watches her cross the room with pride in his eyes. GREAT GRANDDAUGHTER Adam, look at this! As soon as she's gone, Max slowly reaches into his coat pocket and pulls out an envelope. Zev's name is written across it. Max's hands shake almost uncontrollably as he gives the envelope to Zev as slyly as possible. Zev looks at it, confused.
Now stand up -- tell everyone -you're tired -- and go read it. Zev follows Max's orders. He rises from the couch and announces to the room...
I am tired. I am going to bed now.
CHARLES, 60s, Zev's son, approaches and gently helps his dad to the bedroom. It's only a small gesture but you can see how much he loves his father.
It's okay, Dad. It's been a long week.
INT. BEDROOM - NIGHT - MOMENTS LATER

Zev sits down on the bed and stares at the envelope. He opens it and pulls out A STACK OF HUNDRED DOLLAR BILLS. Even if he had control of all his faculties, he'd still be bewildered. Zev then pulls out a TICKET and a THICK STAPLED LETTER, which he begins to read. He does not read it out loud but the power of the words are clear by the look on his face. After a beat, he stands up and walks to his closet. He bends down and retrieves a small, black TOILETRY BAG...
INT. LIVING ROOM - 3:00 A.M.
Zev exits his bedroom, carrying only the toiletry bag.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
He shuffles across the room and lets himself out the front door.
INT. HALLWAY - NIGHT - CONTINUOUS
Zev walks down the dark, silent hallway.
INT. GROUND FLOOR - NIGHT - MOMENTS LATER

Zev walks toward the lobby. As he gets closer, the glimmering light of security monitors rise from behind the front desk. However, the desk is vacated, with only a guard's jacket draped over an empty chair. Zev walks past the desk, glances at the monitors and proceeds through the front doors, which gently slide open.
EXT. PARKING LOT - NIGHT - MOMENTS LATER
Zev stares into the pitch black parking lot. An early fall breeze blows, sending a slight chill down his spine. After a beat, a TAXI pulls up and Zev gets in.
INT. NEW JERSEY TAXI - NIGHT - CONTINUOUS
The TAXI DRIVER already knows where Zev is going...
Penn Station, right? Yes.
ANGLE FROM ABOVE Max peers through his bedroom window as the taxi takes off and disappears into the night.
INT. MAX'S BEDROOM - NIGHT - CONTINUOUS
Max, wearing pajamas, sitting in his wheelchair, watches as the taillights fade away. After a beat... Got it. SECURITY GUARD (O.C.)
Double White Rev (08-15-14)

The bedroom door opens behind Max; kneeling there is the jacket-less SECURITY GUARD, holding a PUSH PIN. Max's aide hurries into the room with a glass of water. I'm so sorry, Mr. Rosenbaum. I don't know how the door got locked. It's okay. Max turns from the window and looks at the security guard. Thank you.
EXT. ROUTE 495 - NIGHT
Zev stares out the window at the early morning New York City skyline -- a million lights glow across the Hudson. The taxi wraps around Route 495, toward The Lincoln Tunnel.
Double White Rev (08-15-14) INT. TRAIN PLATFORM - MORNING

Zev shuffles his way to his train car. Other TRAVELERS -and their bags -- make their way onboard. The station puts Zev at unease. He's nervous. The sounds of trains coming and going and whistles piercing the air echo inside his head, building to a crescendo... CONDUCTOR
49 Lake Shore Limited! Last call! 49 Lake Shore Limited! Last call!

Zev wipes off his sweaty forehead, gathers his courage, and steps onboard. We voyeuristically watch through the train's windows as Zev makes his way down the aisle and finds his assigned seat. He then takes out the letter and begins to read it again. After a beat, he removes a pen from his blazer and crosses something off the page.
INT. TRAIN - DAY - MOMENTS LATER
TYLER, 12, shaggy hair, plops down in the seat next to Zev. He immediately looks out the window and waves at his MOTHER, late 30s.
Is that your mother? Yeah.
She's not coming with you?
No. I'm going to see my dad. (MORE)
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
You're traveling alone?
No, I'm with my brothers. They're sitting over there.
Tyler points across the aisle at his OLDER BROTHERS. I see.
Are you going to Cleveland too? Yes.
Why don't you fly? It's faster. I don't know.
That's not a good reason.
Zev laughs for the first time. Mood swings.
You're a real whipper snapper, aren't you? Why don't you fly to Cleveland?
Three plane tickets is too expensive. That's what my mom and dad said.
That is a good reason. (beat) I am not flying because my friend told me to take the train in the letter he wrote me.
Zev motions at the letter as he folds it up and places it in his blazer's inside pocket.
See, that's a good reason. (beat) My name's Tyler.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Nice to meet you, Tyler. My name's Zev.
That's a strange name. It means "wolf" in Hebrew.
Cool. I wish I had an animal name.
Zev smiles at Tyler as the train's engine begins to rumble.
INT. TRAIN - DAY - LATER

Tyler, wearing headphones, plays on a PSP. Zev sits in his chair, reading the letter again. After finishing the last page, he folds it up and places it in his jacket pocket. He then stares out the window as the train rumbles along, parallel to a highway, watching as houses streak by. He takes it all in...
INT. JEWISH SENIOR HOUSING COMMUNITY - DAY
It's the shift change. The STAFF cross paths at the entrance, exchanging greetings.
INT. ZEV'S APARTMENT - DAY
Paula sits on the reclining chair, knitting her sweater; she's made considerable progress since yesterday morning. She checks her watch and then KNOCKS on Zev's bedroom door.
Mr. Guttman, time to wake up. (beat) It's breakfast time.
Paula knocks again and then opens the door.
INT. ZEV'S BEDROOM - DAY - CONTINUOUS
The room is empty.
Mr. Guttman? (MORE)
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Paula looks in the bathroom.
Mr. Guttman?
INT. DIRECTOR'S OFFICE - DAY - LATER
The DIRECTOR, 50s, wearing a suit and tie, sits across from Charles and REBECCA, Zev's daughter-in-law. Charles stirs in his seat.
We've notified the police and they've issued a silver alert and a reverse 9-1-1 for the community. Our staff is doing all we can-CHARLES I don't understand how this could happen?
Charles stands and paces the room, anger percolating. Between the death of his mom and now this, it's a lot to deal with.
I can't tell you how sorry we are. We try our best to keep an eye on
We pay you to do more than try your best.
Is there anything we can do to
Besides paying you to lose people?
Does your father have a credit
(off Rebecca's nod) You can tell the company to call you if he uses it.
We'll get right on that.
The director takes a breath, ready to deliver even harder
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Your mother's nursing aid told me your dad's been very confused recently. (MORE)
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
She said he's been having a hard time remembering people's names and what day of the week it is-CHARLES He has dementia. That's why we moved my parents here.
I understand that. However, your father's condition might be much further along than we thought. When seniors start to wander-CHARLES This is bullshit. You're just trying to cover yourself.

I know this isn't easy to hear but it's very common following the death of a spouse for more signs of neurocognitive disorders to emerge. Have you noticed that your father's been having mood swings? Or that he rarely plays the piano anymore?
This floats in the air. Rebecca turns to Charles.
I've noticed a few things, Charles. We were just so busy with your mom that I guess I dismissed them.
Charles sits back down, the truth weighing on him. He runs his hands through his hair.
Rebecca reaches out and holds Charles's hand.
When we find your father we're going to need to reassess his living situation as he may require the more supervised environment of our assisted living wing.
Charles and Rebecca nod their understanding.
And let me assure you, we'll have him back here before you know it. (MORE) (MORE)
In these situations, people never wander too far.
INT. TRAIN - DAY

Zev is fast asleep in his chair. Tyler sits next to him, still playing on his PSP, as do his brothers across the aisle. The force of a train passing in the other direction SHAKES the train and rattles the window. Zev wakes up with a shudder, bathed in sweat. Ruth?
Zev, clearly confused, looks at Tyler.
Adam, where is your grandmother? (beat) Adam, where is your grandmother?
Tyler pauses his game and takes out one of his earphones.
Were you talking to me, Zev?
I asked where your grandmother is.
One is in New York. One is in Ohio.
Tyler unpauses his game and begins to play again.
Where is your sister?
I don't have a sister.
Stop playing with that thing and answer me seriously!
Zev grabs the PSP from Tyler; Tyler's a little bit scared.
Are you okay, Zev?
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Zev looks around. He's just as scared as Tyler.
(timidly) Where are we?
We're on a train. The word "train" scares the hell out of Zev. To where?
Cleveland?
ZEV
You're going to see your friend.
What friend?
The one who wrote you the letter in your pocket.
Tyler points at Zev's pocket.
Can I have my PSP back?
Zev hands Tyler his PSP as he takes out the letter from his pocket and begins to read it. After a moment, a tear streams down his face...
(to himself) Ruth is dead.
That does it, Tyler is officially creeped out.
Zev, I'm going to sit with my brothers for a little bit.

Tyler gets up and goes across the aisle, lodging himself between his brothers. Zev pays him no attention as he continues to read the letter. After a beat, Zev takes out the pen and writes "Read Letter" on the outside of his left hand.
Double White Rev (08-15-14) EXT. CLEVELAND LAKEFRONT STATION - DAY
Zev exits the station, carrying only his toiletry bag. He looks around, seemingly lost. Excuse me.
TYLER'S DAD (O.C.)
Zev turns around and sees Tyler's Dad, Tyler and his two brothers.
My son said you were sitting next to him on the train and that you
seemed a little confused.
Yes, I'm sorry about that.
There's no need to apologize. We just want to make sure you're okay.
Yes. Thank you. I'm okay. I appreciate your concern.
Do you need help getting somewhere?
No. Someone is meeting me here. My friend said he will have a sign with my name.
Well, we'll help you find him. The taxi stand's over there.
The group walks together toward the TAXIES and sure enough, standing there is a DRIVER with a sign: "MR. GUTTMAN"
Is your last name Guttman?
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Looks like you're riding in style.
Tyler's dad gives Tyler a look, meaning everything's okay.
Alright, guys, let's hit the road! Bye, Zev! Goodbye.
INT. TOWN CAR - DAY - MOMENTS LATER
The driver helps Zev into the backseat and then gets into the front.
I was told you've got two stops.
Zev looks at the letter for guidance.
Yes, that is correct.
The driver turns on the car and takes off. Zev takes the pen out of his pocket and crosses something off in the letter.
EXT. MINI-MALL - DAY
The car pulls into the half empty parking lot of a run-of-themill mini-mall.
INT. TOWN CAR - DAY - CONTINUOUS
The driver pulls up to a shop; we can't see what it is.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
First stop.
The driver gets out of the car and opens Zev's door.
EXT. BUDDY'S G & A - DAY - CONTINUOUS

Zev exits the car and stands with the driver in front of BUDDY'S G & A (Guns and Ammo). Although this is suburbia, it blends in with the mattress store and Chinese restaurant it's squeezed between. Nobody bats an eye around here when someone wants a firearm. Zev takes his toiletry bag and walks into the store.
INT. BUDDY'S G & A - DAY - CONTINUOUS

The GUN SHOP OWNER stands behind a counter, stacking cases of bullets. Inside the glass display case is every type of handgun on the market. On the wall are rifles of different calibers, some with scopes and some without. The bell on the front door jingles and Zev walks in. Evening.
Good evening.
Zev shuffles his way to the counter. How can I help you?

My friend recommended your shop to me. Well, tell your friend we appreciate the recommendation. What are you looking to pick up? Just something small. Sure thing. Can I see your driver's license? (MORE)
Double White Rev (08-15-14)

Zev reaches into his pants' pocket and pulls out his wallet. He removes his license, which we can't see, and hands it to the owner who begins to enter Zev's information into a database on his computer: Beachwood. Nice town.
The owner continues to type and click on a mouse...

What are you doing with my license? I'm entering your information into the FBI background check database. What do they look for? A bunch of stuff. Whether you're a convicted felon. Been involved in a crime of violence. If you've been dishonorably discharged from the army. If you're mentally competent. If you've been convicted of more than one DUI within the last five years. If you're a fugitive or currently charged with a felony.
A fugitive? If you're running from the law.
Zev half grins.
Do I look like I can run?
The owner submits the information online. A throbber appears on the screen and starts to circle, processing Zev's information in the NICS database.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Let me show you some of our smaller handguns.
The owner gives Zev the quick run down on guns, while pointing at them through the display case.

I would have guessed that but we get all types in here. The owner pulls out a Smith & Wesson 22. A lot of beginners go with a 22. It's light. Easy to use. Very little recoil. That's what my friend suggested. In my opinion though, you might as well buy a BB gun if you're gonna go with a 22. Frankly, it's just not gonna get the job done in an emergency. I'm not trying to give you the hard sell but for about five hundred more you could get something with real stopping power.
The owner takes out a GLOCK.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
The owner hands the gun to Zev. He stares at it, not
We watch Zev from the end of an aisle of hunting gear. ANGLE ON GUN SHOP OWNER The owner glances at the computer. The throbber still spins.
Is it easy to use? Simple.

Can you please write down the instructions for me? Sure thing. I can show you how to use it too. That would be good. But please write it down for me. Sometimes I forget things. We all do. That's no problem.
The owner looks at the computer screen. Zev's been approved by the NICS E-check system. It's that easy.
Zev has to think about this for a second.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Double White Rev (08-15-14) INT. CLEVELAND HOLIDAY INN - LATE AFTERNOON

A CLERK, 40s, Indian, sits behind the desk, surfing the internet. Through the front glass doors we can see the town car pull up. Zev pays the driver with cash from the envelope and then gets out of the car. After a beat, Zev enters the lobby with his toiletry bag and a Buddy's G & A shopping bag. Mr. Guttman?
Your friend called before and said you'd be arriving soon. Your room's all ready for you.
The clerk smiles and holds up the room key. How much do I owe?
You can pay when you check-out. Oh, and I booked your taxi for the morning, like your friend asked. Thank you.
INT. HOTEL ROOM - NIGHT

The room is quiet with only faint sounds of a neighboring room's television seeping through the walls. Slivers of neon light beam through the cracks in the curtain. Zev lays on the bed, reading the letter. The empty box for the Glock and a case of BULLETS is opened next to him. The gun, fully loaded, sits on the bedside table. The phone RINGS. Zev answers it.
Hello? (beat) Yes, I'm okay. Thank you, Max. (beat) I will. Have a good night.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)

Zev hangs up the phone, folds the letter and places it under the Glock. He then stands and slowly begins to disrobe. In the room's half light, traces of his once impressive physique can be seen. He removes his shirt and pants, carefully folding them with military precision. Besides his singlet and briefs, the only thing Zev wears is a wedding ring and a gold, Star of David necklace.
INT. BATHROOM - NIGHT - MOMENTS LATER

Zev enters the bathroom. He sits down on the toilet seat and looks around the room. His eyes lock on the SPRINKLER on the ceiling; something about it bothers him. After a moment Zev shakes off the thought, puts down the drain in the tub and begins to draw a bath. As the water comes out, he lets out the slightest sigh of relief. With his left arm, he reaches out to test the water's temperature revealing a small, crudely written, blue, CONCENTRATION CAMP TATTOO on his forearm. It reads: 98814 For survivors, a shower will always elicit fear, no matter how much time has passed.
INT. BATHROOM - NIGHT - LATER

Zev is sound asleep in the tub. If his skin wasn't wrinkled from age, it would be now from sleeping in the bath. Zev's eyes open. He focuses on the white porcelain tub and tries to get his bearings. However, he doesn't know north from south in this unfamiliar bathroom.
(beat) Ruth?!
Silence.
INT. BEDROOM - NIGHT - CONTINUOUS
In the darkness of the hotel room, Zev, who has a towel wrapped around his waist, is a silhouette, backlit by the fluorescent bathroom lights emanating from behind him.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Ruth? Can you turn on the light?

I can't see. Ruth? Please. Please, Ruth. Silence. Zev brushes up against the wall, looking for a light switch. After a few seconds of flickering, a light turns on. Zev looks around the room, completely at a loss. He sees the Glock on the bedside table. This is the last thing he expected to see. He approaches the gun and carefully picks it up. He turns it over, examining it. He then notices the message on his left hand -- "Read Letter" -- and sees the envelope on the bed. He sits down, picks up the letter and begins to read. After a beat, a tear streams down his cheek. He continues to read. After another moment he stops and LIFTS HIS LEFT ARM, examining his tattoo. He couldn't possibly forget about that, could he?
EXT. HOUSE - DAY

An OHIO TAXI pulls up to the front of a two story, postwar, raised ranch style house, covered by aluminum siding. Zev exits the taxi and slowly shuffles up the driveway; under his arm he carries his toiletry bag, the only place his Glock could be. The taxi waits in front. Zev holds a black metal railing as he climbs the house's six front steps. As he gets closer, he can faintly hear the white noise of a VACUUM on the other side of the door. He takes a piece of paper out of his pocket, which has the name "Rudy Kurlander" written on it with the address of where he's at. Zev rings the bell. While he waits for somebody to answer, he studies the paper. After a beat, he rings the bell again. Finally the vacuuming stops and the front door opens. A CLEANING LADY, 20s, stands there, armed with a Hoover. Hello. Is Rudy Kurlander home?
Double White Rev (08-15-14)

The cleaning lady motions toward the dark stairs as she holds the door open for Zev. Zev steps inside and begins to walk down, into the darkness. After a few steps, the vacuum roars back to life behind him.
INT. DOWNSTAIRS HALLWAY - DAY - CONTINUOUS

Zev takes a moment to look at some PICTURES ON THE WALL. He stares intensely at the OLD MAN in all of the photos. The sound of a television, mixed with a loud, deep belly laugh can be heard from the end of the hallway. Zev grasps his toiletry bag under his arm and walks toward the noise. The television and laughter grow louder. Sweat starts to accumulate on Zev's forehead.
INT. TELEVISION ROOM - DAY - CONTINUOUS

A black and white episode of LAUREL AND HARDY plays on a plasma television. RUDY KURLANDER, 88, fat, sits on a wornin couch, enthralled by the TV. ZEV (O.C.) Are you Rudy Kurlander? Rudy looks over and sees Zev standing in the shadows, HOLDING THE GLOCK at his side. The light from the TV flickers on Zev's face.
(composed) Yes, I am Rudy Kurlander. Who are you?
Rudy speaks with a very thick Eastern European accent.
Rudy turns off the TV. Without this light source, the room is draped in darkness except for the slivers of light peering through the blinds.
Do I know you?
Zev is trying to figure that out.
Please stand by the window.
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
I am not going to do anything until you tell me who you are and why you're here.
Let us not argue. Please, stand by the window.
Obediently, Rudy pushes himself up off the couch. He picks up his cane and walks five feet to the window. Now what?
I need to see light on your face.
Rudy moves a few blinds to the side. A shard of bright light illuminates his eyes. He squints as the sunshine irritates his cataracts.
Turn your head.
Who are you?!
Please, do not yell. (beat) Do as I say. Turn your head.
Rudy turns his head, staring through the blinds at the Yellow Birches in the backyard. Zev studies the old man's profile.
Are you German? After a moment.
Ja. Sind Sie Deutscher? Subtitle: "Yes. Are you German?" Yes.
(MORE)
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
Rudy talks more confidently in his native tongue. Zev understands the German but continues to speak in English.
How old are you?
Achtundachtzig.
Were you in the German army? (off Rudy's nod) What did you do?
Das liegt in der Vergangenheit. Subtitle: "It's in the past."
Zev shakes his head, irritated.
Please speak in English. I don't like German. What did you do?
It was almost seventy years ago. I was very young.
Answer the question.
Rudy lets this process.
Are you a Jew?
Yes. (beat) Were you at Auschwitz? No.
We are too old for lies so please do not.
I am not lying. (beat) (MORE) (MORE)
Double White Rev (08-15-14)
I did not know about Auschwitz until after the war. (beat) I was in North Africa.
North Africa?
I was with the Desert Fox. I was with Rommel.
Zev tries to wrap his head around this.
Do you have proof?
Yes. Over there.
Rudy points at the bar on the side of the room.
Show it to me.
Rudy walks behind the bar and, using his cane as a brace, slowly gets down on his knees -- this is not an easy task for an old man.

When I became a U.S. citizen, the immigration officer asked me if I had ever associated with the Nazi government of Germany or if I worked at a concentration camp.
